entries.title: The future of data centers
entries.subtitle: An examination of data center infrastructure requirements, challenges, and policy considerations for supporting AI growth and economic competitiveness.
entries.abstract: This article analyzes the critical role of data centers in enabling artificial intelligence applications across multiple sectors. The authors examine infrastructure challenges including energy and water consumption, critical mineral requirements, workforce shortages, permitting barriers, and electric grid investment needs. The analysis addresses financial costs, job market impacts, national security considerations, and geographical distribution strategies necessary for ensuring sustained digital economy transition while maintaining U.S. international competitiveness.

entries.title: Eli Lilly, InSilico Strike AI Drug Discovery Deal
entries.abstract: Eli Lilly announced a drug discovery partnership with Hong Kong-listed InSilico valued at up to $2.75 billion, comprising a $115 million upfront payment and milestone-based compensation. Under the agreement, InSilico will grant Lilly exclusive worldwide rights to oral treatments developed using its Pharma.AI artificial intelligence platform across multiple therapeutic areas. This deal extends an existing partnership begun in 2023 and reflects a broader trend among global pharmaceutical companiesâincluding AstraZeneca and Takedaâestablishing licensing agreements and major investments in China's increasingly competitive drug innovation sector.

entries.title: To repeal or not to repeal is not the question for Section 230
entries.abstract: This piece examines the debate surrounding Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act and argues against its repeal as a solution to online harms. The author contends that repealing Section 230 would not effectively address concerns about harmful content and platform accountability because much of the problematic speech remains constitutionally protected. Instead, the author proposes targeted structural reforms including enhanced transparency requirements, privacy protections, researcher access safeguards, and user data portability that would address underlying concerns without incentivizing platforms to suppress lawful speech or concentrating further power among large platforms.

entries.title: Walmart Is Putting Digital Labels that Change Prices Instantly on Every Store Shelf in America
entries.subtitle: Walmart's rollout of digital shelf labels creates technological infrastructure that could enable dynamic pricing in grocery retail, raising consumer protection concerns.
entries.abstract: Walmart announced plans to deploy digital shelf labels (DSLs) across all U.S. stores by end of 2026, replacing paper price tags with remotely updatable digital displays. While marketed as a labor-efficiency measure, DSLs remove the operational friction that previously constrained frequent price changes, making dynamic pricing technically feasible in grocery retail. The article examines how this capability differs from existing dynamic pricing in airlines and hotels, discusses the distinct social contract governing grocery pricing, and references consumer backlash against Wendy's dynamic pricing announcement as evidence of market resistance to such practices.

entries.title: The Game Theory of How Algorithms Can Drive Up Prices
entries.subtitle: Learning algorithms may achieve tacit collusion and high prices without explicit coordination or threat mechanisms, posing novel regulatory challenges.
entries.excerpt: Machine learning algorithms used for dynamic pricing can unexpectedly converge on collusive outcomes without any explicit coordination Ã¢ÂÂ learning to implement mutual retaliation strategies similar to human cartels. The findings expose a gap in antitrust enforcement frameworks designed to detect explicit agreements, not emergent algorithmic behavior.
entries.abstract: This article examines how machine learning algorithms used for dynamic pricing can unexpectedly converge on collusive pricing outcomes without explicit programming or coordination. Recent research demonstrates that algorithms can learn to implement implicit threats and mutual retaliation strategies similar to human cartels, and that even benign profit-optimizing algorithms may produce supracompetitive prices.
entries.preview: A clear-eyed explainer on how pricing algorithms can converge on collusive equilibria without any explicit coordination Ã¢ÂÂ and why existing antitrust frameworks are poorly equipped to respond.
